survivors.title: require_green_ci.sh checks the latest CI run's conclusion but never its headSha — stale-green run can gate-pass an untested/newer commit
survivors.claim: The pass-9 compensating control for unenforceable branch protection (#CI-1) runs `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1` and passes if that single newest run has status=completed, conclusion=success. It never compares that run's headSha to the branch's current HEAD (or the PR merge SHA). `gh run list` returns the newest run by createdAt regardless of which commit it ran on (confirmed: the headSha field is present in the JSON but unused). Failure mode: contributor pushes commit A → CI goes green; pushes breaking commit B; in the registration window between `git push` of B and GitHub creating B's workflow run, `--limit 1` still returns A's completed green run, so the gate exits 0 and B merges un-gated. Even outside that race, the documented usage `scripts/require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n>` decouples the branch whose run is inspected from the PR/SHA actually merged.
survivors.why_real: Verified directly: `gh run list --branch main --workflow ci.yml --limit 3 --json status,conclusion,headSha,createdAt` returns runs ordered newest-first with distinct headShas (a49b3f6, 6e3d6b4, 57d9355), and the script's jq filter (`.[0].status`, `.[0].conclusion`) reads only the newest run's status/conclusion — headSha is fetched by no field and compared to nothing. This is the SOLE merge gate per docs/MERGE-POLICY.md (branch protection is 403 on this plan), so a stale-green pass is a real merge-safety hole in the exact control built to stop silently-red CI from merging — the failure mode pass-9 cited as having recurred across passes #161/#162/#166/#167/#168.
survivors.suggested_fix: Fetch headSha alongside status/conclusion and require it to equal the SHA being merged. E.g. `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 --json status,conclusion,headSha` and assert `headSha == $(git rev-parse "$branch")` (or the PR's merge-head SHA) in addition to status=completed/conclusion=success; fail closed if the latest run's SHA != current HEAD (CI for this commit hasn't completed yet).

refute.reasoning:
Verified directly against the code at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/require_green_ci.sh:19-30. The finding holds on every checkable point.

The script's only data fetch is `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 --json status,conclusion -q '.[0].status + " " + (.[0].conclusion // "none")'`. It requests ONLY `status,conclusion` — `headSha` is not even in the `--json` field list, so the finding's claim that headSha "is fetched by no field and compared to nothing" is accurate (the finding's prose mentioning headSha "present in the JSON but unused" is slightly imprecise about the field list, but the substantive claim — that no SHA is compared — is exactly correct and in fact stronger). The gate passes purely on `status == "completed" && conclusion == "success"` of `.[0]`, the newest run by createdAt.

There is no comparison of the inspected run's commit to `git rev-parse "$branch"` (HEAD) or to the merged PR's head/merge SHA. Both failure modes are real:
1. Registration race: after `git push` of breaking commit B and before GitHub creates B's workflow run, `--limit 1` still returns commit A's completed-green run, so the gate exits 0 for an untested B.
2. Decoupling: the documented usage `scripts/require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n>` inspects a branch's latest run but merges a PR/SHA with no enforced linkage between them.

docs/MERGE-POLICY.md confirms this script is the SOLE, mandatory merge gate ("Until protection is available, the merge gate is procedural + scripted") because branch protection returns HTTP 403 on this free private plan. It explicitly cites the recurrence the finding references — #161/#162/#166/#167/#168 merged with conclusion=failure. A stale-green pass therefore reopens the exact merge-safety hole this control was built to close.

Out-of-scope rationales do not apply: this is not dataset-engine logic, not an import-guarded optional backend, not a committed transcript, not on-box data sovereignty, and not documented honest-scope. It is a security/merge-safety control with a genuine gap. The fix is a one-line SHA equality check; its absence is a real production defect. Verdict: STANDS.

survivors.title: _hostname_patterns redacts the bare machine hostname with a trailing \b but no leading boundary and as a substring — over-redacts/corrupts transcripts on nodes with a short or common hostname
survivors.claim: The machine-hostname rule compiles `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` with re.IGNORECASE and NO leading word boundary, so the bare hostname matches as a substring inside longer tokens. Demonstrated: a hostname `host` redacts inside `localhost` (`local[REDACTED]`) and inside `host.docker.internal`; a hostname `[REDACTED]` redacts inside `[REDACTED]-backups` (`[REDACTED]-backups`). On a CI runner or contributor machine whose hostname is a common substring (e.g. `host`, `ubuntu`, `runner`, `build`), legitimate transcript content gets silently mangled. Unlike the operator-name-token rule (which also omits the leading \b but is explicitly bounded by manual corpus verification of all 256 occurrences) and the handle rule (which requires the whole distinctive letter sequence), the hostname rule has neither protection and pulls names from socket.gethostname/platform.node/scutil ComputerName (a human-set name that can be a common word).
survivors.why_real: Confirmed by executing the exact compiled pattern against sample text (see substring demonstration). The rule runs at module import on every capture/commit (`_HOSTNAME_RES = _hostname_patterns()`, line 251) on whatever node runs the pre-commit hook. On this node the hostname is the distinctive `[REDACTED]` so live exposure is low, but the logic is node-generic and the only test (test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames) uses a deliberately distinctive `buildbox-7`, so the over-redaction path is untested. Severity is low because committed transcripts are intentionally scrubbed corpus, not runtime data, and the current node's hostname is distinctive.
survivors.suggested_fix: Anchor both sides for the bare hostname form, e.g. `r"\b" + re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"`, and/or raise the length/distinctiveness guard for the BARE-token redaction (keep substring matching only for the .local FQDN form, which is unambiguous). Add a regression test with a hostname that is a substring of legitimate content (e.g. machine name `host`) asserting `localhost`/`host.docker.internal` survive.

refute.reasoning: Verified directly against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:238. The machine-hostname rule compiles `re.escape(b) + r"(?:\.local)?\b"` with re.IGNORECASE and NO leading word boundary, exactly as claimed. Empirically reproduced the substring over-redaction: hostname `host` -> `local[REDACTED]` in `localhost` and `[REDACTED].docker.internal`; hostname `[REDACTED]` -> `[REDACTED]-backups` in `[REDACTED]-backups`. The only guardrail is `len(b) < 4` (line 235), which does NOT block common CI/contributor hostnames: `host`(4), `build`(5), `runner`(6), `ubuntu`(6) all pass and all mangle legitimate tokens (`local[REDACTED]`, `re[REDACTED]`, `[REDACTED]-latest`, etc.). The comparison to sibling rules is accurate: the repo-handle rule (lines ~176-189) requires the WHOLE distinctive letter sequence (>=8 chars with a bounded `[\s._-]*` separator class) so single common tokens like CISO are not redacted, giving it protection the hostname rule lacks. The test claim also holds: the sole test test_scrub_redacts_machine_and_namespace_hostnames (tests/test_scripts.py:199) monkeypatches a deliberately distinctive `buildbox-7` that has no common-substring collision, so the over-redaction path is untested. The pattern is built at module import (line 251, `_HOSTNAME_RES = _hostname_patterns()`) and runs on every capture/commit on whatever node runs the pre-commit hook, with the name sourced from socket.gethostname/platform.node/scutil ComputerName (a human-set, possibly-common value). Every checkable assertion in the finding is confirmed by the actual code and reproduced behavior. Severity-low framing (scrubbed corpus, distinctive `[REDACTED]` on the current node) is appropriate but the defect is real and node-generic; nothing refutes it.

survivors.title: require_green_ci.sh pre-merge gate never compares the CI run's commit SHA to HEAD — certifies merges for untested commits (stale-run hole)
survivors.claim: The compensating pre-merge gate that substitutes for unenforceable branch protection (#CI-1) queries only `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1` and checks that run's status/conclusion. It never reads or compares the run's `headSha` against the local/remote tip it is about to merge. So the gate reports 'CI is green — safe to merge' based on whatever the most-recent run for the branch was, regardless of which commit that run tested.
survivors.why_real: Concrete, reproducible failure mode: (1) push commit A, CI goes green; (2) add a NEW commit B (or amend/force-push) on the same branch; (3) run `scripts/require_green_ci.sh` before CI for B has started (or while B's run is still in_progress so the latest *completed* success is still A's). `gh run list --limit 1` returns A's green run, the script prints 'safe to merge' and exits 0, and the operator merges B — code CI never ran on. I confirmed the exact mechanism: `gh run list --branch main --workflow ci.yml` exposes a per-run `headSha`, and right now the local pass-10 HEAD is 17b4eb52 while `require_green_ci.sh main` would certify green off run a49b3f68 — a DIFFERENT commit. This is precisely the un-gated-red-merge class the script was written to prevent (the doc admits CI 'went silently red across several hardening passes'), but the implementation leaves the door open for the stale-/wrong-SHA case. Branch protection that would normally enforce SHA association is unavailable on this plan, so this script is the ONLY gate.
survivors.suggested_fix: Fetch the run's `headSha` alongside status/conclusion (`--json status,conclusion,headSha`) and require it to equal the commit being merged: `git rev-parse "$branch"` (or `origin/$branch` after a fetch). Fail closed if the latest completed run's headSha != the tip SHA, with a message like 'latest green CI ran on <sha>, but HEAD is <sha> — push and wait for CI'. Also treat status!=completed as not-green (already done) AND refuse when no run exists for the exact SHA.

refute.reasoning:
Verified against /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/require_green_ci.sh (lines 17-30). The gate runs `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 --json status,conclusion -q '.[0].status + " " + (.[0].conclusion // "none")'` and decides solely on status==completed && conclusion==success. It never extracts or compares the run's headSha against the tip being merged (no `git rev-parse`, no headSha read anywhere in the merge path — grep over scripts/ and .github/ confirms only this single un-SHA-checked query exists).

The reproducible stale-/wrong-SHA mechanism is real and I confirmed it empirically: `gh run list --branch main --workflow ci.yml --json status,conclusion,headSha` returns the latest completed run as conclusion=success with headSha=a49b3f685c..., while the local pass-10 HEAD is 17b4eb5279... (branch harden/pass-10). So `require_green_ci.sh main` would print "CI is green — safe to merge" / exit 0 based on a49b3f68's run, certifying a merge of a DIFFERENT commit that CI never validated. The classic failure flow holds: push A (green), add/amend B, run the gate before B's run completes — `--limit 1` returns A's success and the gate green-lights B.

Not refutable on scope grounds: the script's own header and ci.yml:232 establish that branch protection is unavailable (HTTP 403 on a private free-tier repo) and that this script is the ONLY compensating gate against the un-gated-red-merge class that previously let CI go silently red across passes. A gate that certifies untested commits defeats its sole purpose. This is a genuine high-severity production defect in the merge-safety control. Correct fix: pull the latest run's headSha and require it equals the local/remote tip being merged, and treat in_progress/absent runs for the target SHA as non-green rather than falling back to an older success.

survivors.title: Operator private LAN/gateway IP + interface + ISP vendor leak into committed transcripts — scrubber has no IP/network-topology rule
survivors.location: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:40-58 (_SECRET_PATTERNS) and :193-245 (_hostname_patterns); leaked corpus e.g. transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d.jsonl and .../tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt
survivors.claim: The scrubber redacts email/username/display-name/GitHub-handle/hostname but has NO rule for IP addresses or network topology. The operator's real machine private LAN IP [REDACTED], the LAN gateway [REDACTED], the active interface en1, and the ISP/router vendor 'Peplink cellular' leaked into committed transcripts verbatim via the output of routine diagnostic shell commands (lsof -i connection table 'TCP [REDACTED]:49227->160.79.104.10:443 (ESTABLISHED)', `route get default` dump 'gateway: [REDACTED] / interface: en1', and ifconfig). [REDACTED] is verifiably THIS node's current address: `ipconfig getifaddr en0` returns exactly 192.168.50.134. These are a distinct PII class from the host/name/email/handle classes prior passes covered.
survivors.why_real: Falsifiable and verified: (1) `grep -niE 'ip|192|168|inet|lsof|netstat|route|gateway' scripts/capture_transcripts.py` matches only docstring/path text — there is literally no IP-redaction code path. (2) `ipconfig getifaddr en0` on this node returns [REDACTED], and `ifconfig | grep 'inet 192.168'` confirms [REDACTED] — so the committed value is the operator's live LAN address, not a test fixture. (3) The leaking file tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt contains 20 '[REDACTED]' markers (hostname/username WERE scrubbed on the same lsof lines) while the IP survives — proving the scrubber ran and simply lacks coverage, not that the file was skipped. (4) The .50.x/.99.x test-fixture IPs in src ([REDACTED], [REDACTED]) are intentional ADR-0003 non-loopback examples and are NOT the concern; the concern is the en0-confirmed real-host triple {LAN IP, gateway, interface} plus the named ISP/router brand. A LAN IP + gateway + ISP-equipment vendor narrows the operator's physical network and, combined with the established-connection peer IPs, is operator/infrastructure de-anonymization data in a corpus the README explicitly intends to publish.
survivors.suggested_fix: Add a network-topology redaction pass to _scrub. Minimum: derive the node's own non-loopback IPv4/IPv6 addresses at runtime (e.g. socket.getaddrinfo(socket.gethostname(), None) and/or psutil/`ipconfig getifaddr`/`ifconfig` parsing) plus the default-gateway IP, and redact those exact literals (they are stable, unambiguous identifiers — analogous to the existing hostname rule). Provide an ASSAY_SCRUB_IPS env (comma-separated) for the gateway and any other infra IPs that aren't bound to a local interface. Also add the router/ISP vendor token(s) (e.g. 'Peplink') to the ASSAY_SCRUB_NAMES-style handling so the equipment brand is masked. Do NOT blanket-redact all RFC1918 IPs — that would destroy the legitimate ADR-0003 loopback/non-loopback test fixtures (127.0.0.1, [REDACTED], [REDACTED]) which are intended example values; redact only the runtime-derived operator-specific literals. Then re-run capture over the existing corpus to scrub the already-committed leaks.

refute.reasoning:
Finding STANDS. Every falsifiable claim verified against the actual code and committed corpus:

1. NO IP/network-topology redaction rule: scripts/capture_transcripts.py:40-58 (_SECRET_PATTERNS) covers sk-ant/gh/AWS/bearer tokens, env-var secrets, email, /Users|/home username, and project-slug username. Lines 61-245 add username (_username_pattern), display-name (_display_name_patterns), repo-handle (_repo_handle_patterns), and hostname (_hostname_patterns). There is NO regex for IPv4 addresses, gateways, interfaces, or vendor names. Grep for ip/192/168/inet/lsof/gateway/peplink matched only docstring/code-structure text, no redaction path.

2. The leaked IP is THIS live node's address: `ipconfig getifaddr en0` returns exactly 192.168.50.134.

3. The IP and topology leaked verbatim into COMMITTED (git-tracked) transcripts. `git grep -l "[REDACTED]" -- transcripts/` returns 4 tracked files including the named transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-...jsonl and .../tool-results/bv1ufu6gh.txt. Gateway [REDACTED] and "Peplink" also present and tracked.

4. Scrubber-ran-but-IP-survived confirmed: bv1ufu6gh.txt contains 20 [REDACTED] markers (hostname/username were scrubbed) while [REDACTED] and [REDACTED] survive (1 occurrence each in that file). Adjacent lines show /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-grc and -Users-[REDACTED]-hc-grc redactions on the same diagnostic output — proving the scrubber executed and merely lacks coverage, not that the file was skipped.

5. Not a test fixture: `git grep [REDACTED] -- src/ tests/` returns nothing; the value appears only in the live-host diagnostic transcript output.

6. README/transcripts/README.md explicitly intend to publish transcripts as provenance.

Defenses considered and rejected: (a) "documented honest-scope" — transcripts/README does caveat local paths and email, but the scrubber is the ACTIVE automatic mitigation that advertises operator-PII coverage and is extended every pass; this is a genuine coverage gap in that mitigation. Network topology (LAN IP + gateway + interface + ISP/router vendor) is a distinct PII class never covered. (b) "test-fixture IPs" — the finding correctly pre-empts this; the en0-confirmed live triple is the concern, not the ADR-0003 examples. Every prior PII pass (#F-024 email/username, #J-002 display-name, #J-008 handle, #P9-PII-1 hostname) was adjudicated a valid defect under the identical pattern; this is consistent. Finding holds.

survivors.title: libpq PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE env vars bypass the loopback guard on the Postgres checkpointer (graph state leaves the box)
survivors.claim: require_local_uri validates only the connection STRING. libpq merges connection parameters from the environment (PGHOSTADDR, PGHOST, PGPORT, PGSERVICE+PGSERVICEFILE) for any parameter not explicitly present in the string. The default DSN 'postgresql://localhost:5432/assay' has an explicit host but NO hostaddr, so libpq uses the env var PGHOSTADDR as the actual TCP target while 'host=localhost' is used only for TLS/SNI. Setting PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 (or PGSERVICE pointing at a service file with a remote host, or PGHOST when the DSN form 'dbname=x' — which the guard accepts — carries no host) makes psycopg/libpq connect off-box even though require_local_uri passed. Confirmed: psycopg.conninfo.conninfo_to_dict('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay') yields {'dbname','host','port'} with no 'hostaddr', so PGHOSTADDR is merged at connect time.
survivors.why_real: This defeats the module's own stated invariant ('graph state must not leave the box', checkpoint.py:161) under standard, documented libpq environment-variable semantics — no malicious server or exotic input required, just an env var an operator/CI/upstream process can set. The guard re-audit explicitly targets 'remaining bypass' cases; this is a concrete data-egress path the string-only check cannot see. No code anywhere scrubs or pins PG* env vars (grep for PGHOST/PGHOSTADDR/PGSERVICE in src returns nothing outside comments).
survivors.suggested_fix: In get_postgres_connection_string (or before building ConnectionPool), neutralize libpq env overrides: pop/empty PGHOSTADDR, PGHOST, PGPORT, PGSERVICE, PGSERVICEFILE from the env psycopg will read, OR parse the merged conninfo via psycopg.conninfo.conninfo_to_dict with env applied and re-run is_loopback_host on the effective host/hostaddr, OR pass an explicit hostaddr=127.0.0.1 (and host=localhost) in the conn dict so env cannot supply it. Treat PGSERVICEFILE/PGSERVICE as off-box unless the resolved service host is loopback.

refute.reasoning:
I attempted to refute SEC-10-1 but could not. The finding holds and is a real production data-egress defect.

Empirical proof (psycopg 3.3.4 + libpq, run in the project venv against the default DSN postgresql://localhost:5432/assay):
- psycopg.conninfo.conninfo_to_dict('postgresql://localhost:5432/assay') => {'dbname','host','port'} with NO 'hostaddr' key, exactly as the finding claims. require_local_uri (src/assay_engine/_local.py:110) only inspects the string's authority hosts and any host/hostaddr query/DSN tokens; it never reads the environment.
- Control connect (env -u PGHOSTADDR, host=localhost:5432): libpq connected to the local Postgres instantly ("CONNECTED?!"), proving a live local server.
- With PGHOSTADDR=203.0.113.5 (and again 192.0.2.1, both non-routable TEST-NET): the SAME DSN no longer reaches the local server — it times out after the full connect_timeout (2-3s) attempting the REMOTE address. This proves libpq used PGHOSTADDR as the actual TCP target while host=localhost was reduced to a TLS/SNI label, i.e. the connection left the box despite require_local_uri passing.

No mitigation exists: grep over src/ finds zero handling of PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE/PGPORT (no os.environ.pop/del, no hostaddr pinning in _DEFAULT_PG or the ConnectionPool kwargs at checkpoint.py:275). The team is aware libpq dials hostaddr — tests/test_persistence.py:110 even comments "libpq dials hostaddr" — but only guards hostaddr present IN THE STRING, not the env var that supplies it when absent. The pool then passes the bare conn_str to ConnectionPool(conn_str, ...), so libpq performs the env merge unguarded.

Out-of-scope rebuttals do not apply: this defeats the module's own explicitly stated invariant ("graph state must not leave the box", checkpoint.py:161 / ADR-0003 data sovereignty), requires no malicious server or exotic input — just a standard documented libpq env var an operator/CI/parent process can set — and the guard re-audit explicitly targets remaining string-bypass cases. This is a concrete, real, env-driven off-box egress path the string-only check structurally cannot see.

Severity high (data sovereignty / state egress) is appropriate. Files: src/assay_engine/_local.py:110-165 (string-only guard), src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:157-164 and :275 (default DSN with no hostaddr passed to ConnectionPool).

A correct fix would pin connect-controlling env-merge parameters in the resolved conninfo (e.g. set hostaddr explicitly when host is loopback, or clear/validate PGHOSTADDR/PGHOST/PGSERVICE before connecting), not merely validate the string.

survivors.title: SIGTERM handler swallows the signal when a prior C-installed handler makes getsignal() return None
survivors.claim: _handler captures prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM) at install time and, on SIGTERM, chains only if callable(prior) or re-delivers the default only if prior == signal.SIG_DFL. signal.getsignal() is documented to return None when the SIGTERM handler was installed from non-Python (C) code. In that case prior is None: neither branch runs, _handler flushes and RETURNS, and because signal.signal replaced SIGTERM's default terminate action with this flush-and-return, the process does NOT terminate on SIGTERM. docker stop / kill / k8s then hang until SIGKILL escalation (~10s) — the exact hang the code comment (#K-REL-1) claims to prevent. The comment enumerates SIG_DFL and SIG_IGN as the only non-callable cases ('SIG_IGN stays ignored') and omits None.
survivors.why_real: Verified the code path by reading lines 159-163: the if/elif has no else and does not cover prior is None. Python's signal.getsignal docs explicitly state it returns None if the handler was not installed from Python. The failure mode (SIGTERM swallowed -> container/orchestrator stop hangs until SIGKILL) is concrete and matches the documented hazard the branch was written to avoid; it is simply not covered for the None disposition. The test suite (test_observability.py) only exercises the callable and SIG_DFL cases.
survivors.suggested_fix: Treat None (and any non-callable, non-SIG_IGN disposition) the same as SIG_DFL: change `elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL:` to also re-establish the default and re-deliver when prior is None, e.g. `elif prior in (signal.SIG_DFL, None):`. Add a regression test that monkeypatches signal.getsignal to return None and asserts the handler resets to SIG_DFL and calls os.kill (re-delivery), mirroring the existing SIG_DFL test.

refute.reasoning:
Verified against the actual code at src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:150-163. The finding holds on every checked point:

1. CODE BRANCH CONFIRMED: _handler captures `prior = signal.getsignal(signal.SIGTERM)` (line 148), then after flush/shutdown does `if callable(prior): prior(...)` / `elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL: reinstall+redeliver` (lines 159-163). There is no `else`, so when `prior is None` BOTH guards are false: the handler flushes and returns, having already replaced SIGTERM's default terminate action — the signal is swallowed and the process does not die.

2. None DISPOSITION IS REAL AND DOCUMENTED: `signal.getsignal.__doc__` on this box states it returns `None -- if an unknown handler is in effect` (i.e., a SIGTERM handler installed from non-Python/C code). Confirmed `None != SIG_DFL` and `None != SIG_IGN`, so neither branch matches.

3. FAILURE MODE MATCHES THE COMMENT'S OWN HAZARD: the #K-REL-1 comment explicitly says returning without re-delivery would make `docker stop`/`kill`/k8s hang until SIGKILL escalation. That is exactly what happens in the uncovered None case. The comment enumerates only SIG_DFL and SIG_IGN as the non-callable cases and omits None — confirming the gap is an oversight in code that was specifically written to prevent this hang.

4. TEST GAP CONFIRMED: tests/test_observability.py only stubs getsignal to return SIG_DFL (lines 215, 262) and exercises the callable + SIG_DFL paths; the None disposition is untested, exactly as the finding states.

5. REACHABILITY: low but non-zero — the deployment pulls opentelemetry-exporter-otlp (pyproject.toml:54) which can use the gRPC C-core; C extensions / gRPC / embedded interpreters can install a SIGTERM handler that makes getsignal() return None. This justifies the finding's own LOW severity rather than refuting it.

Not out-of-scope, not already-mitigated, not dataset-specific, and not intentional (the comment proves intent was to cover all non-callable dispositions). It is a genuine, narrow correctness gap. STANDS. Minimal fix: add an `else` (prior is None / SIG_IGN/anything non-callable) that re-establishes SIG_DFL and re-delivers SIGTERM so termination is never swallowed — and add a getsignal()->None test case.

survivors.title: Provenance to_records/from_records roundtrip silently fails verification for any set/frozenset payload value
survivors.claim: A ProvenanceTrail entry whose payload contains a set or frozenset (at any nesting depth) cannot be serialized with to_records() and re-verified with from_records()/verify_records(): the chain hash check fails with ProvenanceError('content was altered ... hash mismatch'). Reproduced: ProvenanceTrail().record('k','s', tags={'a','b','c'}); from_records(trail.to_records()) raises ProvenanceError. Also reproduced with a nested set ({'inner': {7,8}}). Tuple-only payloads roundtrip fine; only sets/frozensets break.
survivors.why_real: The genesis-rooted entry digest (_digest -> canonical_json) canonicalizes a frozenset as {"__set__": [...]} (canonical_plain line 50-51). But unfreeze() (used by entries_to_records) converts frozenset -> a plain sorted list, and from_records re-freezes that list into a tuple (freeze() line 99-100). canonical_plain then serializes the tuple as a bare [...] list, NOT {"__set__": [...]}, so the recomputed digest differs from the stored entry_hash and verify_records fails closed. record(kind, summary, **payload) is a public API and the as_recorder() gate path forwards a study-controlled evidence mapping that can legitimately hold a set; the module docstring (lines 16-28) and entries_to_records docstring (211-225) explicitly market to_records/from_records as the persist-and-recheck path for an audit trail crossing a trust boundary. A trail that verifies in-process but fails after persistence defeats the tamper-evidence guarantee (a benign serialize/reload looks identical to tampering), and an operator cannot distinguish real corruption from this false positive.
survivors.suggested_fix: Make the serialized form round-trip-faithful for sets. Either (a) have entries_to_records preserve set-ness with a tagged marker that from_records re-materializes into a frozenset before re-hashing, or (b) compute the entry digest over the already-unfrozen (JSON-native) payload on BOTH record and verify paths so canonicalization is identical pre/post serialization, or (c) reject set/frozenset payload values at record() time with a typed ProvenanceError directing callers to use a list. Add a roundtrip test covering a set-valued and nested-set-valued payload.

refute.reasoning:
Reproduced exactly against the real code (.venv Python 3.14, PYTHONPATH=src).

Repro results:
- ProvenanceTrail().record('k','s', tags={'a','b','c'}) -> in-process trail.verify() PASSES, but from_records(trail.to_records()) raises ProvenanceError: "provenance entry 0 content was altered, or the wrong key was used (hash mismatch)".
- Nested set {'inner': {7,8}} -> same failure.
- frozenset payload -> same failure.
- Tuple control (vals=(1,2,3)) -> roundtrip OK. Confirms only sets/frozensets break.

Root cause confirmed directly:
- Digest path: _digest -> canonical_json -> canonical_plain (_canonical.py:50-51) serializes a frozenset as {'__set__': ['a','b']}. Verified: canonical_plain(frozenset({'a','b'})) == {'__set__': ['a','b']}.
- Serialize path: to_records/entries_to_records (provenance.py:226-240) uses _frozen.unfreeze (_frozen.py:153-161), which thaws frozenset -> a plain sorted list. from_records (267-295) rebuilds the ProvenanceEntry, whose __post_init__ -> freeze_mapping -> freeze (_frozen.py:99-100) converts that list into a TUPLE. verify_records (243-264) then recomputes the digest; canonical_plain(('a','b')) == ['a','b'] (bare list, line 48-49) — NOT {'__set__': [...]}. The two digests differ, so hmac.compare_digest fails closed.

Reachability / scope:
- record(kind, summary, **payload) is public; as_recorder() (162-188) forwards a study-controlled evidence mapping that can legitimately hold a set.
- No guard rejects set-valued payloads. The _frozen.freeze docstring explicitly advertises set support ("sets -> frozensets"), and the provenance module docstring (16-28) and entries_to_records docstring (211-225) explicitly market to_records/from_records as the persist-and-recheck path crossing a trust boundary. No docstring scopes sets out.
- No existing test covers a set/frozenset payload roundtrip (test_provenance.py uses only scalar/string payloads).

Impact is exactly as described: a trail that verifies in-process silently fails verification after a benign serialize/reload, making a correct persisted trail indistinguishable from a tampered one — defeating the tamper-evidence guarantee the module exists to provide. This is a real production defect, not mitigated, not out-of-scope by design. Finding STANDS.

survivors.title: from_records raises raw AttributeError on a non-mapping payload field, violating its typed-error contract
survivors.claim: from_records() with a record whose 'payload' value is present but not a Mapping (e.g. a list) raises a bare AttributeError("'list' object has no attribute 'items'") instead of the module's typed ProvenanceError. Reproduced with a record carrying payload=['not','a','map'].
survivors.why_real: The from_records comment at lines 277-281 (#G-012) explicitly states a malformed/incompatible trail record must surface as the module's typed error, not a raw exception, so a caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt trail file covers it. The existing guard only checks for MISSING fields; a present-but-wrong-typed payload bypasses it and freeze_mapping() inside ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__ throws AttributeError (not caught by the ValueError/TypeError/RecursionError handler around ProvenanceEntry construction at lines 155-158). A corrupt or attacker-supplied trail file thus crashes a loader that correctly only catches ProvenanceError, turning a fail-closed integrity path into an unhandled crash.
survivors.suggested_fix: In from_records, validate that each record's 'payload' is a Mapping before constructing ProvenanceEntry (raise ProvenanceError naming the record index and offending type), or widen the ProvenanceEntry construction except-clause to include AttributeError. Add a from_records test with a non-mapping payload asserting ProvenanceError.

refute.reasoning:
Confirmed by direct reproduction. Running from_records([{...,'payload':['not','a','map'],...}]) against the actual code (src/assay_engine/provenance.py) raises a raw `AttributeError: 'list' object has no attribute 'items'` rather than the module's typed ProvenanceError.

Root cause matches the finding exactly:
- from_records (lines 282-292) constructs ProvenanceEntry directly with payload=r["payload"] and — unlike the in-module write path _record_locked (lines 145-158) — does NOT wrap construction in a try/except that converts ValueError/TypeError/RecursionError into ProvenanceError. There is no protective handler around the from_records construction at all.
- ProvenanceEntry.__post_init__ (lines 65-66) calls _frozen.freeze_mapping(self.payload).
- freeze_mapping (lines 129-139) does `{k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}` with no Mapping type check, so a list payload throws AttributeError on `.items()`. (Even if it were caught like _record_locked does, AttributeError is not in that handler's (ValueError, TypeError, RecursionError) tuple — so it would escape regardless.)

The typed-error contract is real and explicit: the comment at lines 277-281 (#G-012) states a malformed/incompatible trail record must surface as the module's typed error, not a raw exception, precisely so a caller catching ProvenanceError to handle a corrupt trail file is covered. The guard implementing that contract only checks for MISSING required fields; a present-but-wrong-typed payload bypasses it entirely. This is a genuine inconsistency within the integrity/loader fail-closed path: a corrupt or attacker-supplied trail file with a non-mapping payload crashes a loader that correctly only catches ProvenanceError, turning a fail-closed path into an unhandled crash.

This is not out-of-scope (it is core provenance integrity code, not an optional backend or dataset-specific logic), not already-mitigated (reproduced on HEAD), and is a real defect against a documented contract. Medium severity is appropriate (loader robustness / typed-error contract violation, not a confidentiality/integrity bypass of the chain itself). Finding STANDS.

refuted_ids.why:
The finding accurately describes the CI mechanism (.github/workflows/ci.yml:142-157 regenerates all three locks with `uv pip compile ... --exclude-newer "$(cat .uv-exclude-newer)"` = 2026-06-14T00:00:00Z and diffs against committed locks), but it does not hold as a real production defect.

It is REFUTED on three grounds:

1. Not a real defect — inherent, documented contract of any live-index point-in-time lock. `--exclude-newer` filters by PyPI upload-time against the LIVE index, so any such scheme (uv, pip-compile, poetry, npm) carries the residual that upstream index history is not a frozen snapshot. The repo already went the maximal reasonable distance: uv pinned to 0.11.21 (#F-031), resolution date pinned as a single source of truth in .uv-exclude-newer read by both CI and scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh (#K-OPS-1), and full hash pinning (`--generate-hashes`). Eliminating the residual entirely requires a private mirror / vendored wheels — a different architecture, out-of-scope by design for an on-box engine resolving against public PyPI. There is no committed bug in ci.yml to fix.

2. Author concedes it is sound today and fail-closed. The finding states all three locks are currently in sync (reproduced locally with pinned uv) and that this is "not a security exposure," self-rated low. The triggering event (PyPI unyank, or delete+reupload of a pre-cutoff release) is an exotic mutation of normally-immutable history. Hash pinning further narrows it: a delete+reupload of a DIFFERENT artifact changes the sha256 and is caught by pip-audit --require-hashes / install rather than silently flipping the resolved version; the only true resolver-flip window is an unyank of a version yanked at lock time (uv only falls back to yanked versions when nothing else satisfies the constraint — a narrow case).

3. The implied remediation already exists and is documented. A spurious red is recoverable by re-running scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh and committing, which is the exact documented path. So the worst case is rare, externally-triggered, self-announcing operational noise on a fail-closed gate — not a production defect in the code under review.

The finding correctly describes a real code asymmetry: require_loopback_url (_local.py:61-69) uses urlparse(url).hostname directly with no backslash-in-authority check, whereas require_local_uri (:138-145) explicitly rejects '\\' in netloc (audit #D6). I verified the parser differential: urlparse('http://evil.com\\@localhost/').hostname == 'localhost', so the backslash-userinfo form is ACCEPTED by require_loopback_url.

However, the finding is REFUTED as a production defect on two grounds:

1) No real defect today (the finding itself concedes severity is low and no off-box connection results). The ONLY consumer of require_loopback_url is BULK_BASE_URL (ASSAY_BULK_BASE_URL), used by exactly two clients (seam.py:367 langchain_ollama/httpx, and seam.py:718 urllib.request.urlopen). I verified urllib's urlsplit resolves the backslash form to hostname 'localhost' (RFC-3986 last-@ userinfo, no \\->/ normalization); httpx.URL behaves the same way (RFC 3986). So both real consumers connect to localhost — no data leaves the box. The guard plus the client both land on the same host, so the guard is not bypassable into an off-box connection with the actual consumers.

2) The finding's cited mechanism does not apply. It claims a WHATWG-normalizing client "used by the Claude CLI path" (undici/fetch) would read host 'evil.com'. But the Claude CLI path is the HIGH_STAKES tier (_high_stakes_complete, seam.py:415+, via claude_agent_sdk), which connects to Anthropic's hosted API via subscription OAuth — it never consumes BULK_BASE_URL or require_loopback_url. grep confirms BULK_BASE_URL has no other consumers. There is no WHATWG-normalizing client in the codebase that ingests this URL.

This is therefore a pure defense-in-depth / consistency nit (an acknowledged gap that 'only bites a future/alternate client'), not a real production defect — the two guards differ in robustness but the less-robust one guards a URL whose only real clients agree with its parsing, and the at-risk client type does not consume it. Out of scope as a production defect; default-to-REFUTED applies.

Refuted. The finding has two halves; the NaN half is factually wrong against the actual code, and the inf half is not a real production defect.

NaN half (false): The finding claims RUN_JSON_DEADLINE "can still surface nan depending on argument order." Verified the real code: ALL five float_env call sites (seam.py:95,101,104,105,111) put the constant FIRST — max(1.0, float_env(...)) and max(0.0, float_env(...)). I confirmed empirically that max(1.0, float('nan')) == 1.0 (NaN comparisons are False so max keeps the first arg). So NaN is caught at every call site; the deadline never becomes NaN. The finding's "depending on argument order" caveat invokes a max(nan, 1.0) ordering that does NOT exist anywhere in the file. The NaN claim is refuted.

inf half (technically true, not a defect): float('inf') parses and max(1.0, inf) == inf is real. But: (1) It requires an operator to deliberately type ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT=inf — not a plausible typo of "120" (a real fat-finger like "12O" raises a cleanly-named ValueError as designed). (2) inf is a valid float, not malformed input, so the helper's documented contract (NAME malformed input) is not violated. (3) The finding's "hang forever" ignores the design's independent guards: the inline comment at seam.py:96-99 states the wall-clock timeout is explicitly NOT the real guard — BULK_NUM_PREDICT (line 100) caps output tokens, and the inflight pool (lines 225-230) bounds concurrency and rejects on saturation. So output is bounded regardless of an infinite httpx timeout. (4) This is an on-box, single-operator, dataset-agnostic engine (data-sovereignty deployment) where an operator choosing an infinite timeout is choosing that behavior.

No finite/isfinite/isnan check exists anywhere, confirming this was never treated as in-scope. At most this is a trivial low-value robustness nicety; combined with the demonstrably-false NaN claim, it does not hold as a real production defect. Default to REFUTED.
